1: The standard word-event formula — vayehi devar YHWH ʾelai leʾmor — opens a new section. Ezekiel's book is organized around these divine-speech introductions; they mark the beginning of distinct oracular units. The formula occurs over 50 times in Ezekiel, more than any other prophetic book.
2: ben-ʾadam betokh beit-hameri ʾattah yoshev — "son of man, you are dwelling among a rebellious house" — beit hameri (house of rebellion) is Ezekiel's characteristic designation for Israel. Meri (rebellion) is a noun of action derived from marah (to be bitter/rebellious against authority). The phrase ʿeynayyim lahem lirʾot velo raʾu (they have eyes to see but do not see) echoes the Isaianic hardening formula (Isa 6:9-10) and is cited by Jesus in Mark 4:12 and John 12:40.
3: ʿaseh lekha kelei golah — "prepare for yourself exile-gear" — kelei (implements/gear) is a general term for personal equipment. The instruction to pack in front of them during the day and then move out at evening creates the visual narrative of deportation. The sign-act performs the coming exile before it happens.
4: The two stages of the baggage-carrying sign-act — during the day (yomam) and at dusk (beʿerev) — correspond to the stages of exile: the visible departure and the night-flight that Zedekiah will make (2 Kgs 25:4). The dusk/night timing is historically specific to Zedekiah's attempted escape.
5: beqir ḥatsor-lekha vehotseta vo — "dig through the wall before their eyes and go out through it" — the wall-digging sign-act represents the hole in the city wall through which Zedekiah's party escaped. The Hebrew ḥatsar means to dig/pierce; the action symbolizes the forced breach of the city's defenses.
6: bevalayl tatse — "go out at night" — the nighttime departure is the specific circumstance of Zedekiah's flight. The covering of the face (kaseh ʿal-paneikha) is interpreted in v12 as the prince covering his face so as not to see the land with his eyes — a reference to Zedekiah's blinding (2 Kgs 25:7). The sign-act precedes the event it symbolizes by months or years. The term mofet (wonder/portent) for Ezekiel himself is significant: he does not merely announce but embodies the prophetic word.
7: The faithful execution of the sign-act — vaʾaʿas keʾasher tsuvviti (I did as I was commanded) — is Ezekiel's standard response to divine commands. The Hebrew imperfect-consecutive narrative (vav-consecutive + imperfect) creates the sequential action report. The obedient execution is as much a part of the prophetic act as the verbal oracle.
8: Morning brings the word again — vayehi devar YHWH ʾelai beboqer — the divine speech frames the sign-act. The night-departure is followed immediately by the morning-explanation, creating a close prophetic discourse unit.
9: The house of Israel's question — halo ʾamru ʾeleikha beit Yisraʾel beit hameri ma ʾattah ʿoseh — "has not the house of Israel said to you: what are you doing?" — the rhetorical question anticipates their puzzlement and sets up the explanation. The beit hameri (rebellious house) epithet attached to the questioners colors their question with the very attitude that makes the sign necessary.
10: hanasi hamassa hazze biYerushalaim — "this oracle concerns the prince in Jerusalem" — hanasi (the prince/leader) is Ezekiel's preferred term for the Judean king; he rarely calls the king melek (king) in chapters 1-24, possibly to avoid legitimizing Zedekiah's kingship. The term massa (oracle/burden) derives from nasaʾ (lift/carry) — the oracle is a weight carried.
11: Ezekiel as mofet — keʾasher ʿasiti ken yeʿaseh lahem bagolah yelikhu behasher — "as I have done, so it will be done to them; into exile, into captivity they will go" — the identification of the prophet with the people he represents is Ezekiel's distinctive feature. The sign-act has executive force: what Ezekiel mimes, the community will experience. Mofet (wonder) usually denotes a divine act; applying it to the prophet makes the prophet's enacted body a divine instrument.
12: The specific application to the prince — vehanasi ʾasher betokhah ʾel-khetefav yissaʾ baʾalafel veʾel-pi haqqir yiḥtoru lehotseti vo ʿeynayyiv yekhasseh yaʿan ʾasher loʾ yirʾe leʿayin ʾet-haʾarets — the covering of the eyes so as not to see the land with the eye is the predictive element: Zedekiah will have his eyes put out (2 Kgs 25:7) before being taken to Babylon. The sign-act's eye-covering turns out to be a precise prophecy of his blinding.
13: YHWH spreading his net over the prince — ufrassti et-resheti ʿalav venitpas bimetsudati — the hunter/net imagery for divine judgment. The verb paras (spread/stretch) + resheti (my net) + nitpas (captured) traces the divine entrapment. Zedekiah is caught while fleeing (2 Kgs 25:4-5); he will come to Babylon but not see it — the eyes put out before the arrival precisely fulfills "he will not see it."
14: The scattering of all who support the prince — vekhol-ʾagappayu ʾezroh lekhal-ruaḥ — "all who are around him I will scatter to every wind" — ruaḥ (wind/spirit) here means compass-directions ("to every wind" = in all directions). The four winds appear throughout Ezekiel as the scope of divine scattering and gathering (5:10; 37:9).
15: The recognition formula — veyadeʿu ki ʾani YHWH — "and they will know that I am YHWH" — is Ezekiel's most characteristic refrain (appearing ~72 times). The purpose of every judgment and every restoration is the acknowledgment of YHWH's identity and sovereignty. The formula uses the imperfect yadeʿu (they will know) — the knowing is future, when the events confirm the word.
16: The remnant that survives the sword, famine, and pestilence — vehishtarti mehem ʾanshei mispar — "I will leave of them a few men" — mispar (number/count) means "men who can be counted," i.e., a very small remnant. The remnant's purpose: lemaʿan yesapperu kol-toʿavoteyhem baggoyim — they will declare all their abominations among the nations. The scattered remnant becomes involuntary testimony to YHWH's justice.
17: Another word-event formula initiates the new sign-act. The double formula (vv1 and 17) marks chapter 12 as containing two distinct sign-act oracles.
18: benaʿara tokhal laḥmekha uviraʿash vetimhon tishte meimekha — "eat your bread with quaking and drink your water with trembling and apprehension" — the three nouns for fear/anxiety: naʿara (anxiety), raʿash (trembling), timhon (dismay/confusion). The escalating emotional vocabulary describes the physical experience of siege-anxiety. The sign-act mimes the psychology of siege more than its logistics.
19: The interpretation: the land and its fullness will be stripped from the city's inhabitants — letitom eret koʾah tiqqafrethah (she will be stripped of what fills her). The eret koʾah (fullness of the land) is everything the land produces. The expression "because of the violence of all who dwell in it" grounds the economic desolation in moral cause.
20: The ruined-city conclusion — vehaʿarim hanoshabot teḥarovna vehaʾarets shemamah tihyeh — follows the earlier pattern (6:14). The future tenses (teḥarovna, tihyeh) are imperfects of strong prediction. The verse summarizes the geographic scope: not just Jerusalem but all the inhabited cities.
21: A third oracle-unit opens with yet another word-event formula. The focus now is on a specific proverb that was circulating among the exiles and the Jerusalem community.
22: ma hammashal hazze lakhem ʿal-ʾadmat Yisraʾel leʾmor yaʾarikhu hayyamim veʾavad kol-ḥazon — "what is this proverb that you have in the land of Israel: the days grow long and every vision comes to nothing?" — the proverb challenges the credibility of unfulfilled prophecy. Mashal (proverb/byword) represents communal conventional wisdom that has become a form of resistance to the prophetic word. The rhetorical structure quotes the skeptical community directly.
23: YHWH's abolition of the proverb — vehishbatti et-hammashal hazze — the verb shavat (hiphil: cause to cease/abolish) is the same word used for the Sabbath rest. YHWH "Sabbaths" the false proverb — causes it to stop. The new formula to replace it: qarev hayyamim udvar kol-ḥazon — "the days are near and the word of every vision." The deferred fulfillment becomes imminent fulfillment.
24: The paired abolitions — kol-ḥazon shav vemiqsam ḥalaq — "every empty vision and flattering divination" — ḥazon shav (false/empty vision) is opposed to true prophecy; miqsam ḥalaq (flattering divination) refers to the false prophets' technique of smooth answers. Both will be abolished "in the midst of the house of Israel."
25: ki ʾani YHWH adabber ʾet ʾasher adabber davar veyyeʿaseh loʾ timmasheikh ʿod — "for I YHWH will speak the word that I speak and it will be performed; it will no longer be delayed" — the emphatic self-reference (ʾani YHWH, I myself am YHWH) and the repeated adabber... davar (I will speak... word) underscore the absolute reliability of divine speech. Timmasheikh (be drawn out/delayed) from mashakh — the delay is over.
26: A fourth oracle within ch 12 is introduced. The people's second proverb is quoted: haḥazon ʾasher zeh ḥoze liymim rabbim ulnitot shanim ʾaḥerot hu mitnabe — "the vision he sees is for many days; and he prophesies of times far off." This second proverb does not deny prophecy but safely defers it to the distant future, making it existentially irrelevant to the present generation.
27: The oracle that counters the second proverb — the specific content is in vv28. The structure of the chapter (three proverbs addressed in sequence: the vision-always-fails proverb, the imminence replacement, and the far-future safety proverb) represents a sophisticated engagement with the community's prophetic skepticism.
28: loʾ yimmasheikh ʿod kol-divray ki adabber davar veyyeʿaseh — "none of my words will be delayed any longer; the word I speak will be performed" — the chapter closes with the same imminence formula as v25, forming an inclusio around the two-proverb refutation. The word for "none of my words" (kol-divray) is emphatically comprehensive.
